$comment: Every background task in the backend, and how it dies (ADR-CORE-037, ADR-APP-032).
Spawning work creates an entry point: something starts running with nobody above it to report to.
A task that ends quietly — a loop that exits on the first Err, a worker that returns on a closed
channel without a word — is a silent death, and the user's first hint is a feature that stopped
working with nothing in the log.
The crash gate (scripts/lib/crash-gate.mjs, run by `npm run lint`) counts the spawns in every Rust
source file and rejects any it does not find here. Adding a task therefore forces one question to
be answered in writing: HOW DOES THIS ONE DIE? Answer it in `why`.
This file is PROJECT-OWNED: never pinned, never delivered by `governance:update`. A fork inherits
the entries below with the template and maintains its own from there.

src-tauri/src/lib.rs.why: The log bridge (setup): forwards tracing records to the UI log view. On RecvError::Lagged it warns and KEEPS BRIDGING — the receiver is still valid, so the old `while let Ok(..)` loop, which ended the task and silently froze the log view for the rest of the session, is exactly what this entry exists to prevent. On RecvError::Closed the sender is gone (logging is shutting down): it logs that and ends, which is the only way this task is allowed to stop.

src-tauri/src/jobs/queue.rs.why: Two tasks. (1) The dispatcher (spawned once at startup): pulls enqueued job ids and runs up to `concurrency` at a time behind a Semaphore. It ends ONLY when its mpsc channel closes (process shutdown) — logged, not silent — and a semaphore-closed acquire is handled rather than assumed. (2) The per-job worker (spawned per job): runs one ffmpeg child, streams -progress into percent, and on cancel kills the child and removes the partial output. A single job's spawn failure, non-zero exit or wait error becomes JobState::Failed (logged + emitted) — it never takes the worker or the dispatcher down, so one bad file cannot stop the queue.
